lessons.title: Why Version Control Exists
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to identify the core problems that version control solves and explain why it is essential in professional software development.
lessons.notes: Introduce motivation with real-world scenarios (e.g., 'I broke something', 'two people edited the same file'). Use table format to show problems and solutions.

lessons.title: Three Generations of Version Control Systems
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to distinguish between local, centralized, and distributed version control systems and explain Git's advantages as a distributed system.
lessons.notes: Use a timeline or comparison table. Emphasize Git's distributed nature: every clone is a full copy. Mention RCS, SVN, Git explicitly.

lessons.title: Git's Object Model: Blobs, Trees, and Commits
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to explain Git's content-addressed object model and describe how blobs, trees, and commits work together to store project history.
lessons.notes: Make this visual: show blob as raw file content, tree as directory snapshot, commit as named snapshot with parents. Use ASCII diagrams (safe to include in prose).

lessons.title: The DAG: Directed Acyclic Graph of Commits
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to visualize commit history as a DAG and explain how immutability ensures history cannot be lost.
lessons.notes: Show linear history (A->B->C) and branching (B->C, B->D->E). Emphasize that commits are immutable; you can add to history but never rewrite it.

lessons.title: The Three Working Areas
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to distinguish between the working directory, staging area, and repository, and explain how changes flow through these areas.
lessons.notes: Flow diagram is essential: working → staging → repo. Explain why staging area exists: allows selective, logical commits.

lessons.title: Installing Git and Configuring Your Identity
lessons.learning_objective: By the end of this lesson, learners will be able to install Git on their machine and configure their identity for making commits.
lessons.notes: Hands-on: students download from git-scm.com, run `git config --global`. Distinguish global (used for all repos) vs. per-repo (used only for one).
